schools.summary: Gill: this was at the end of the second forty days, after Moses had fallen down before the LORD for the people's sin of the calf; he was to hew the tables and make an ark.

themes: Hew thee two tables of stone like unto the first... and make thee an ark of wood
the covenant restored after the calf
grace triumphing over the breach

schools.summary: Gill: though hewn by Moses, the writing was the Lord's; the very same laws, in the same words without variation, to show they were of God and not Moses; to be put in the ark.

themes: I will write on the tables the words that were in the first tables... and thou shalt put them in the ark
the law God's own and unchanged
kept under the mercy-seat (Heb. 9:4)

schools.summary: Gill: the ark was made (by Bezaleel) of shittim wood, the same as the ark in the holy of holies.
Clarke: refers to the shittim wood (Exod. 25:5).

themes: I made an ark of shittim wood, and hewed two tables of stone like unto the first
the servant's exact obedience
the ark a type of Christ, the law treasured in him

schools.summary: Gill: the same laws, in the same letters.
Ellicott: the words on the second tables were the same as on the first — the ten commandments.

themes: he wrote on the tables, according to the first writing, the ten commandments
the moral law immutable through the breach
fixed and perpetual, the standard unaltered

schools.summary: Gill: he turned from the Lord, out of whose hands he had received the tables.
Ellicott: 'there they be' is 'they continued there'; the permanent ark was made afterward by Bezaleel.

themes: put the tables in the ark which I had made; and there they be
the law's settled and abiding place
treasured under the mercy-seat

schools.summary: Gill: the account of Aaron's death and burial; Eleazar his son ministered in the priest's office in his stead.

themes: there Aaron died... and Eleazar his son ministered in the priest's office in his stead
the office living on though the man was gone
pointing to the priest who continueth ever (Heb. 7:24)

schools.summary: Gill: Jarchi takes Gudgodah to be the same as Horhagidgad; Aben Ezra reads it as a general name including several stations.
Keil & Delitzsch: continue the list of journeys.

themes: From thence they journeyed unto Gudgodah; and from Gudgodah to Jotbath, a land of rivers of waters
God's continued leading from stage to stage
refreshing his people in the desert

schools.summary: Gill: the separation was after the tabernacle was erected; Levi was to bear the ark, stand to minister, and bless in his name.

themes: the LORD separated the tribe of Levi, to bear the ark... to minister unto him, and to bless in his name
the threefold office of consecrated service
a ministry set apart wholly to holy things (Num. 6:23)

schools.summary: Gill: Levi had no part in the division of Canaan, being separated to the sanctuary's service, with no leisure to plough and sow.
Keil & Delitzsch: the LORD is his inheritance.

themes: Levi hath no part nor inheritance with his brethren; the LORD is his inheritance
God himself the portion of his servants
the LORD the portion of mine inheritance (Ps. 16:5)

schools.summary: Gill: he stayed and fasted as long as the first time; the LORD hearkened to him and would not destroy them.

themes: the LORD hearkened unto me at that time also, and the LORD would not destroy thee
the power of the mediator's intercession
pointing to Christ's availing pleading (Heb. 7:25)

schools.summary: Gill: upon Moses' supplication, the Lord was graciously pleased not only to forgive but to order him to go before the people, that they might possess the land sworn to their fathers.

themes: Arise, take thy journey before the people, that they may go in and possess the land
the command to advance sealing the forgiveness
grace restoring the people to their purpose

schools.summary: Gill: this is asked for all the favours bestowed — the forgiveness of their sins and the renewal of the promise of the land.
Henry: a most pathetic exhortation to obedience, inferred from all God's dealings.

themes: what doth the LORD thy God require of thee, but to fear the LORD... to love him, and to serve
the sum of religion in a few words
the reasonable devotion of a forgiven people (Mic. 6:8)

schools.summary: Gill: both the ten commands and all others were to be kept.
Keil & Delitzsch: the commandments commanded 'for thy good.'

themes: To keep the commandments of the LORD, and his statutes, which I command thee this day for thy good
the law given to bless, not burden
obedience and blessing made one (Ps. 19:11)

schools.summary: Gill: the airy and starry heaven, and the third heaven, the heaven of heavens, the seat of the divine Majesty, are the Lord's, made and possessed by him; the earth also, with all in it, is his.

themes: the heaven and the heaven of heavens is the LORD's thy God, the earth also, with all that therein is
the universal sovereignty of God
the God of all yet loving one people (Ps. 24:1)

schools.summary: Gill: though heaven and earth are the Lord's by creation, he had a special regard and peculiar complacency in Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, and chose their seed above all people.

themes: the LORD had a delight in thy fathers to love them, and he chose their seed... above all people
the freeness of electing love
grace singling out the few from the all (Eph. 1:4)

schools.summary: Gill: they were not to content themselves with, nor trust in, outward circumcision, but to be concerned for the circumcision of the heart — the removing of all carnality, sensuality, and hypocrisy, and to be no more stiffnecked.

themes: Circumcise therefore the foreskin of your heart, and be no more stiffnecked
inward renewal beneath the outward sign
the circumcision of the heart by the Spirit (Rom. 2:29)

schools.summary: Gill: he is God of gods — of angels and civil magistrates, so called — his creatures, accountable to him.
Keil & Delitzsch: a great, mighty, and terrible God, who regards not persons.

themes: the LORD your God is God of gods, and Lord of lords... which regardeth not persons, nor taketh reward
majesty joined to perfect impartiality
no respect of persons with God (Acts 10:34)

schools.summary: Gill: God is the patron and defender of the fatherless and widow, who have none to help them, doing them justice himself.
Ellicott: his love to the stranger in giving him food and raiment.

themes: He doth execute the judgment of the fatherless and widow, and loveth the stranger
the great God stooping to the weakest
a Father to the fatherless (Ps. 68:5)

schools.summary: Gill: they were to love the stranger because the Lord loves him, and because they themselves had been strangers.
Keil & Delitzsch: the ground — for ye were strangers in Egypt.

themes: Love ye therefore the stranger: for ye were strangers in the land of Egypt
love taught by God's example and their own affliction
doing to the sojourner as they would be done by (Exod. 23:9)

schools.summary: Gill: the fear of the Lord includes the whole worship of him, external and internal.
Ellicott: in the New Testament this was our Lord's last answer to the tempter — 'him only shalt thou serve' (Matt. 4:10).

themes: Thou shalt fear the LORD thy God; him shalt thou serve, and to him shalt thou cleave
the whole of true devotion to God alone
Christ's answer to the tempter (Matt. 4:10)

schools.summary: Gill: he is the object and matter of their praise, deserving the praises of all his creatures for his perfections, works, and blessings; praise is his due, and comely in his people to give.

themes: He is thy praise, and he is thy God, that hath done for thee these great and terrible things
God himself the theme and ground of praise
glorying not in self but in him (Jer. 9:24)

schools.summary: Gill: the fathers went down with seventy persons in all, and now the Lord had made them as the stars of heaven for multitude, as he promised.

themes: Thy fathers went down into Egypt with threescore and ten persons; and now the LORD thy God hath made thee as the stars of heaven
God's faithfulness to the promise
the word to Abraham visibly fulfilled (Gen. 15:5)
